coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-based fluorescent sensor for rapid detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Pengchao
Wang, Jing
Li, Ting
Pan, Qingbin
Zhu, Linchen
Yu, Feifei
Zhao, Yong-zhen
Liu, Hai-Bo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed a fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria based on magnetic separation, fluorescent probes, and smartphone image processing. A microchannel device was assembled using high-transparency resin and 3D printing technology. This device was combined with a smartphone and an external lens to develop a fluorescent sensor for autonomous detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria. Three fluorescence probes with different fluorescence were synthesized from highly specific aptamers and tetraphenylethylene derivatives. These fluorescent probes can make Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa emit different colors of fluorescence. Using the enrichment performance of molecularly imprinted materials, separation and detection of bacteria can be achieved simultaneously. Finally, with the Red-Green-Blue (RGB) analysis functionality of a smartphone, real-time field detection was realized with a sensitivity of 102 CFU/mL and a detection time of 40 min. This work provides a simple, inexpensive, and real-time sensor for the detection of multiple pathogens in medical diagnostics, food testing, and environmental analyses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic molecular imprinting
Aggregation-induced emission
Multiple detection
Microfluidic
3D printing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of S-MIP, E-MIP, and P-MIP
High-performance liquid chromatography purification of fluorescent probe
Characterization of S-MIP, E-MIP and P-MIP
Adsorption capacity and adsorption kinetics of molecularly imprinted materials
Theoretical design and purification of fluorescent probe
Detection of bacteria by fluorescent probes
Quantitative analysis of bacteria by sensors
Selectivity of fluorescent probes and molecularly imprinted materials
Optimization of bacteria detection based on sensor readings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of sensor device. (A) Synthesis of three magnetic molecularly imprinted materials. (B) Synthesis of fluorescent probes. (C) Schematic diagram of multiple detection of pathogenic bacteria.

Design diagram of 3D model and the construction of the sensor. (A) Two-dimensional plane design of 3D model, (B, C and D) Top, flank and cross-sectional 3D design images of 3D model, (E and F) Top and bottom images of 3D physical model, (G and H) Assemble of sensor device.

Characterization of MIP materials. TEM images of (A) Fe3O4, (B) E-MIP, (C) P-MIP, (D) S-MIP, (E) Fe3O4@SiO2, (F and I) E-MIP with addition of E. coli, (G and H) P-MIP with addition of P. aeruginosa, (H and K) S-MIP with addition of S. aureus. (L) FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, S-MIP, Fe3O4@SiO2, E-MIP, P-MIP. (M) XRD patterns of Fe3O4, S-MIP, Fe3O4@SiO2, E-MIP, P-MIP.

Theoretical design, purification of fluorescent probe and fluorescence response to bacteria. (A) HPLC purification for TPE-apt, (B) HPLC purification for TPE-B-apt, and (C) HPLC purification for TPE-X-apt. (D) Fluorescence spectra of TPE-derivatives and biological probes. (E) DFT-calculated HOMO and LUMO values of TPE derivative (Gaussian 09/B3LYP/6–31 G(d)). Fluorescence spectra of fluorescent probes in the presence of target bacteria, (F) S. aureus, (I) E. coli, and (L) P. aeruginosa. The standard curve between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of the target bacterial solution, (G) S. aureus, (J) E. coli, and (M) P. aeruginosa. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the bacterial incubation time, (H) S. aureus, (K) E. coli, and (N) P. aeruginosa.

The quantitative and qualitative analysis of bacteria and the selectivity of the sensor. After incubation of the bacteria with the fluorescent probe, the fluorescence intensity on the surface of the MIP was measured, (A and D) S. aureus, (B and E) E. coli, and (C and F) P. aeruginosa. The fitting curve of the RGB value and bacterial concentration on the MIP surface, (G) S. aureus, (H) E. coli, and (I) P. aeruginosa. (J) Cross-validating detection of pathogenic bacteria. Selectivity of molecularly imprinted materials and fluorescent probes. (K) Specificity of three imprinted materials (1: E. coli, 2: P. aeruginosa, 3: S. aureus, 4: B. methanolicus, 5: B. subtilis, and 6: B. cereus); (L): Specificity of three fluorescent probes (1: S. aureus, 2: E. coli, 3: P. aeruginosa, 4: B. subtilis, 5: B. cereus, 6: B. methanolicus, and 7: Tris-HCl solution).
Results for the detection of E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa in actual samples by sensor.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-based fluorescent sensor for rapid detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi Province, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi Province 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Shrimp Breeding Engineering Technology Research Center, Guangxi Academy of Fisheries Sciences, Nanning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Shrimp Breeding Engineering Technology Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Shrimp Breeding Engineering Technology Research Center, Guangxi Academy of Fisheries Sciences, Nanning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed a fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria based on magnetic separation, fluorescent probes, and smartphone image processing. A microchannel device was assembled using high-transparency resin and 3D printing technology. This device was combined with a smartphone and an external lens to develop a fluorescent sensor for autonomous detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria. Three fluorescence probes with different fluorescence were synthesized from highly specific aptamers and tetraphenylethylene derivatives. These fluorescent probes can make Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa emit different colors of fluorescence. Using the enrichment performance of molecularly imprinted materials, separation and detection of bacteria can be achieved simultaneously. Finally, with the Red-Green-Blue (RGB) analysis functionality of a smartphone, real-time field detection was realized with a sensitivity of 102 CFU/mL and a detection time of 40 min. This work provides a simple, inexpensive, and real-time sensor for the detection of multiple pathogens in medical diagnostics, food testing, and environmental analyses.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogens are major contributors to foodborne disease outbreaks. Each year, an estimated 600 million people become sick and 420,000 die from eating contaminated food (World Health Organization, 2023). Escherichia coli is a major cause of hemorrhagic enteritis and hemolytic uremia; it is mostly associated with foods such as beef, pork, milk, juice, and vegetables (Pan et al., 2021). Most cases of rapid-onset food poisoning are caused by the enterotoxin produced by Staphylococcus aureus. When minor wounds become infected with S. aureus, life-threatening conditions such as endocarditis, septicemia, pneumonia, osteomyelitis, and arthritis can develop (Shahdordizadeh et al., 2017). Pseudomonas aeruginosa is considered to be one of the most harmful pathogenic bacteria causing intestinal and urinary tract infections in humans, and it has high mortality and morbidity rates (Jia et al., 2021). P. aeruginosa is found in soil, water, air, and food, and is considered one of the major pathogens causing hospital infections.

There are many methods to detect pathogenic bacteria, but they all have drawbacks such as complex pretreatment or long measurement time (Yue et al., 2017). Culture and plate counting methods are currently recognized as reliable for detecting pathogenic bacteria in food and drinking water, but their use is limited by a long time requirement (36–72 h) (Rohde et al., 2017). The enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is widely used because of its high sensitivity and low cost. However, due to its complex, time-consuming washing process and harsh detection conditions, its use is limited. Similarly, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is the most accurate technology to date, but complex sample pretreatment—including DNA purification, PCR amplification, and gel electrophoresis—prevents this method from being widely used (Lungu et al., 2012). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a technology capable of real-time on-site detection of pathogenic bacteria with high sensitivity, simple operation, and low cost.

In recent years, core-shell molecular imprinting technology has often been used in the field of analytical chemistry. With this technology, because the binding site is on the surface of the material, it is easy to prevent nonspecific adsorption and optimize the selectivity and adsorption capacity (Niu et al., 2016). Bio-probes based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE) luminogens (AIEgens) are being rapidly developed in the fields of biosensing and bioimaging for the detection of ions, reactive oxygen species, small biological molecules, and biological macromolecules (Niu et al., 2020). Many aptamers of biological macromolecules have been reported in recent years. Due to their strong specificity, aptamers are often used in biosensors to detect substances like adenosine, adenosine triphosphate, protein, thrombin, and toxin factors (Bi et al., 2017). An aptamer probe has been combined with molecularly imprinted material to detect P. aeruginosa by electrochemical dual signal response with a detection limit of 10 clone forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) (Sarabaegi and Roushani, 2021). However, aptamer probes typically use optical and electrochemical instruments to detect microorganisms, so there is a need for a simpler and less expensive alternative.

In this study, we designed and synthesized a portable fluorescence sensor capable of rapid detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria. E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa were selected as the target bacteria. First, using Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2 as matrix materials, different methods were selected to modify the matrix materials to produce magnetic core-shell molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which could be used to purify, isolate, and identify different pathogens under a magnetic field. Second, a microchannel device was prepared by using high-transparency resin and 3D printing technology. The magnetic MIPs were fixed in the detection area by the magnetic field and then combined with a smartphone and an external lens to form a fluorescence sensor for the autonomous and simultaneous detection of the target bacteria. Aptamers specific to the three bacteria were selected, and tetraphenylethylene (TPE) derivatives with different fluorescence were synthesized via amide bonds to form fluorescent probes that caused the bacteria to emit different colors of fluorescence. Finally, the fluorescent bacteria were continuously injected into the microfluidic device, and the RGB value of each image was calculated by using the image acquisition system of the smartphone and the UV light source for fluorescence excitation to obtain the number of fluorescent bacteria (Fig. 1 ). The detection interval was 102–108 CFU/mL, and the detection time was 40 min. Compared with other detection methods, the proposed sensor has several advantages: it does not require expensive experimental instruments; it is portable and can detect three kinds of bacteria simultaneously, and maintains a wide detection range in short detection time. The comparison between the proposed sensor and a previously reported fluorescence immunosensor is summarized in Table S1.

Whole-cell SELEX technology was used to select the aptamers of the three pathogenic bacteria: S. aureus-aptamer with a sequence of 5′-GCG CCC TCT CAC GTG GCA CTC AGA GTG CCG GAA GTT CTG CGT TAT –C6- NH2-3′ (S-apt) (Cao et al., 2009; Ucak et al., 2020); E. coli-aptamer with a sequence of 5′-CCG GAC GCT TAT GCC TTG CCA TCT ACA GAG CAG GTG TGA CGG –C6-NH2-3′ (E-apt) (Lee et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2020); and P. aeruginosa-aptamer with a sequence of aptamer (5′-CCC CCG TTG CTT TCG CTT TTC CTT TCG CTT TTG TTC GTT TCG TCC CTG C TT CCT TTC TTG-C6-NH2-3′ (P-apt) (Wang et al., 2011). These three aptamers were purchased from Sheng Gong Bioengineering (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Unless otherwise specified, all other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Boca Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sterile water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S-MIP, E-MIP, and P-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, S. aureus, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa were respectively centrifugated and diluted with Tris-HCl (20 mmol/L, pH = 8.5) solution to a concentration of 1.0 × 108 CFU/mL. Then, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2–NH2, and Fe3O4@SiO2–CHCH2 nanoparticles were ultrasonically dispersed in Tris-HCl to prepare a 40 mg/mL suspension. Different functional monomers were used to prepare MIPs (refer to SI). The end-products (MIPs and non-imprinted polymers (NIPs)) were separated and collected by magnets. The MIPs were cleaned once with a 1:1 mixture of 1% sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and 5% acetic acid (HAC), and then three times with deionized water.

TPE-COOH, TPE-B-COOH, TPE-X-COOH (10 mg, Fig. S1), n-hydroxysuccinimide (10 mg), and dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (10 mg) were dissolved in N, N-dimethylformamide (2.5 mL) and shaken at room temperature for 1 h. Next, oligonucleotides dissolved in Tris-EDTA buffer solution (250 μL) were added and shaken overnight in the dark at 37 °C (Lu et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-performance liquid chromatography purification of fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Cold ethanol (1.2 mL) and sodium chloride (3 M, 100 μL) were added to the reaction solution of TPE-derivatives and aptamers. After freezing at −20 °C for more than 60 min, the precipitated oligonucleotides were collected by centrifugation at 4 °C for 15 min (14,000 rpm). The collected materials were dissolved in triethylammonium acetate (TEAA, 0.1 M, 400 μL) and purified by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a C18 reversed phase column. The samples were analyzed by LC-100 series HPLC with GS-120-10-C18 (10 mm, ID × 250 mm) as the chromatographic column. The chromatographic conditions were as follows: flow rate 1.0 mL/min, column temperature 25 °C, detection wavelength 260 nm, mobile phase: (Solvent A: 40% water, Solvent B: 60% acetonitrile, 100% HPLC grade) (Karlsson et al., 2021).

A 3D model of the microfluidic chip mold was drawn using AutoCAD (Fig. 2 A) and exported as an STL file using 3D Studio Max (Fig. 2B and C). The 3D model is shown in Fig. 2D. Then, transparent photosensitive resin was selected as the synthetic material for 3D printing. A 1-mm microchannel structure was fabricated on a 1-cm thick substrate (Fig. 2E and F). The integrated sensor structure was built by combining a USB interface lens, a 365-nm UV lamp, and a smartphone image processing application (Fig. 2G and H). Preliminary data processing was performed using the lens shooting interface application (Fig. S2A) and image RGB processing application (Fig. S2B).

The experiment required that the three MIP types be evenly distributed; therefore magnets were added to the bottom to fix the MIPs. The slow flow rate required for sample entry through the microchannel ensured adequate detection time. After the bacteria and molecular probe were fully incubated, the sample was added, and the excess liquid was removed after the liquid was completely drained. Under the irradiation of a 365-nm UV lamp, the color of each hole was captured using a lens, and the RGB values of the image were processed with the RGB processing application to establish the correlation between the concentration of the bacterial solution and the RGB values. During the experiment, the distance between the UV lamp and the lens was fixed to ensure data accuracy. The environment was dark and free from external natural light interference. To ensure data reliability, the average RGB value of five points (the center point and four points equidistant from the center point) in each image was calculated for experimental processing.

After the mixed bacteria were diluted with PBS solution, milk, drinking water, and fruit juice were diluted 1000 times, added, and cultured for 1 h. The liquid to be tested after co-culture with the fluorescent probe was added to the sensor sampling port, and the supernatant was removed after adsorption for data collection. Bacteria were treated qualitatively and quantitatively according to the RGB values of the images (Hao et al., 2021). The bacteria adsorbed by different MIPs were desorbed, and the plate counting measurement was performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S-MIP, E-MIP and P-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Particle size and morphology of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, S-MIP, P-MIP, E-MIP, and functional groups were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The Fe3O4 particles were spherical and mostly uniform in size (Fig. 3 A). After modification with SiO2, Fe3O4@SiO2 was found to have a typical core–shell structure, wherein the SiO2 shell layer completely covered the surface of Fe3O4 particles. Compared with Fe3O4, the Fe3O4@SiO2 microspheres had a regular spherical shape and smooth surface (Fig. 3E) (Liao et al., 2021). The sizes of prepared P-MIP (Fig. 3B) and E-MIP (Fig. 3C) were similar to the size of the Fe3O4@SiO2 substrate material, but both of the MIPs' silica layer was slightly larger. S-MIP had an obvious dopamine layer compared with Fe3O4 (Fig. 3D). Three MIPs were photographed by electron microscopy after adsorbing bacteria, indicating that the rod-shaped E. coli aggregated around multiple E-MIPs (Fig. 3F), the rod-shaped P. aeruginosa were adsorbed around multiple P-MIPs (Fig. 3G), and the circular S. aureus gathered around multiple S-MIPs (Fig. 3H). By further amplifying the TEM images of the three MIPs, the outline of the rod-shaped E. coli (Fig. 3I), the rod-shaped P. aeruginosa (Fig. 3J), and the circular S. aureus (Fig. 3K) could be clearly seen, and the results verified the specific adsorption of MIP to bacteria (Xie et al., 2019; Zhong et al., 2018). Fig. 3L shows that the band at 580 cm−1 is the Fe–O characteristic absorption band of Fe3O4, and there are different intensities in all curves (Lv et al., 2018). The characteristic bands of S-MIP prepared from Fe3O4 at 1612 cm−1 and 1502 cm−1 may indicate that the surface of Fe3O4 was successfully modified by dopamine (Mumtaz et al., 2017). The asymmetric stretching vibration band of Si–O is 1092 cm−1; the symmetric stretching vibration band of Si–O is 800 cm−1; the bending vibration band of Si–O is 462 cm−1; and the strong and broad absorption band of –OH on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2 is 3418 cm−1. These characteristic bands indicate that SiO2 was successfully loaded on the surface of the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Cai et al., 2020; Che et al., 2017). A band appeared at 1630 cm−1, indicating that APTES was successfully modified (Zhang et al., 2018). The band at 1100 cm−1 indicated that the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2 was successfully modified with vinyl functional groups (Azodi-Deilami et al., 2013). Six characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 could be clearly seen from the XRD pattern (Fig. 3M), and the positions of the peaks were 2θ = 30.38°, 35.53°, 43.09°, 53.48°, 57.09°, and 62.52°. The crystal plane indices were (220), (311), and (400), differing from those of the JCPDS standard spectrum, namely (422), (511), and (440) (Lee and Lu, 2015). The results demonstrate that the characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 were the same, but the intensity was lower, indicating the crystal form of Fe3O4 still existed, but its surface was modified with different polymers, thereby attenuating the peak intensity (Xie et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity and adsorption kinetics of molecularly imprinted materials
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, MIPs were photographed by a fluorescence microscope after the adsorption of bacteria to study the adsorption capacity and adsorption kinetics of MIPs on target bacteria. By using three different imprinting methods to imprint the three bacteria, it was found from Fig. S3 that dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was used as the functional monomer of imprinting S. aureus, acrylamide (AM) and methacrylic acid (MAA) were used as the functional monomer of imprinting E. coli, and AM and N, N' -methylene diacrylamide (MBA) were used as the functional monomer of imprinting P. aeruginosa, the fluorescence intensity of the three MIPs surfaces was higher. The fluorescence intensity of MIP adsorption gradually increased with the increase of the bacterial concentration (Figs. S4A, S4B, and S4C). Under the experimental conditions, from 102 to 108 CFU/mL, no saturation of the surface was observed even in the highest concentration of bacteria, indicating that the binding capacity of the prepared polymeric surface was sufficiently high for its intended purposes. The adsorption capacity of NIP, however, was not directly related to the concentration of the bacterial solution. Therefore, we selected 108 CFU/mL as the adsorption concentration for the subsequent experiment (Bezdekova et al., 2020; Masoumi et al., 2016). The fluorescence intensity of MIPs increased in time-dependence manner when the incubation time was varied from 0 to 40 min. After that, the fluorescence intensity began to decline and then plateau, this suggests that binding time of S-MIP: 40 min (Fig. S4D), E-MIP: 40 min (Fig. S4E) and P-MIP: 30 min (Fig. S4F) is sufficient to perform the isolation process and no extension of time is required. Whereas the adsorption capacity of NIP was not correlated with time (Figs. S4 and S5). It can be seen from Fig. S6 that the fluorescence intensity on the surface of MIP increased with the increase of the concentration of the bacterial solution, and the adsorption capacity of MIP was far greater than that of NIP, providing further evidence that the adsorption capacity of MIP was positively correlated with the concentration of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical design and purification of fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The aim of this experiment was to demonstrate the different fluorescence of three TPE derivatives using time density functional theory (TD-DFT). TPE molecules were modified to obtain TPE derivatives with different fluorescence properties. If multiple derivatives were identified as having the same electron-donating groups, the fluorescence emission spectra of the compounds could be changed by introducing different electron-absorbing groups. The theoretical calculation of the experiment was preliminarily carried out with GaussView software. HPLC was used to verify that TPE derivatives and aptamers produced new substances in the amide reaction solution after the reaction, and a new fluorescence spectrum was obtained after the purification of substances, which further verified the synthesis of fluorescent probes.

By comparing the TD-DFT of TPE, TPE-COOH, TPE-B-COOH, and TPE-X-COOH, it was found that the LUMO-HOMO energy gap (ΔEL-H) from TPE to TPE-X-COOH decreased from 4.1181 eV to 3.0939 eV, as shown in Fig. 4 E. This indicates that the maximum excitation wavelength redshifted and the fluorescence was different (Xu et al., 2017). The peak emergence times of three fluorescent probes were analyzed using liquid chromatographic data: TPE-apt, 13.8–20.0 min (Fig. 4A); TPE-B-apt, 20.5–26.8 min (Fig. 4B); and TPE-X-apt, 20.5–27.0 min (Fig. 4C). Through the different retention time of the probe, aptamers, and TPE derivatives, three fluorescent probes were purified by HPLC. The products were further confirmed by high resolution mass spectrometry (Figs. S13, S14, and S15), and the resulting fluorescent probe was used in subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 4D, TPE and TPE-B–COOH–aptamer showed a blue shift, and TPE-X-COOH ligated on aptamer showed a red shift. This could be because the ligation of aptamer affected the electron-withdrawing ability of the electron-withdrawing groups on the three compounds, therefore the fluorescence spectra of the probe and the TPE derivative were different from each other (Wu et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of bacteria by fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe was weak in the absence of the target bacteria. Due to the good water solubility of DNA molecules, TPE-oligonucleotide probes can be easily dissolved in water. As shown in Fig. 4F, I, and 4L, upon addition of the bacteria, the aptamer recognized the bacteria, resulting in the aggregation of TPE derivative molecules on the surface of bacteria; these molecules, due to their AIE properties, led to an increase in fluorescence intensity. Thus, the TPE-oligonucleotide probe has a weak fluorescence emission in the absence of bacteria, and the fluorescence intensity increases with increasing concentration of the bacterial solution. The recognition ability of the fluorescent probe for bacteria was reflected by the linear fit of the fluorescence intensity (F) and the logarithm of the target bacterial concentration (C). The bacterial concentration range was 0–108 CFU/mL, and the following linear regression models were calculated: F = 2351.1013 + 993.95698 Lg C, R2 = 0.98257 (TPE-apt, Fig. 4G); F = 2026.96922 + 816.90571 Lg C, R2 = 0.98027 (TPE-B-apt, Fig. 4J); and F = 2503.21811 + 585.526048 Lg C, R2 = 0.95870 (TPE-X-apt, Fig. 4M). The fluorescence intensity reached the maximum value at 40 min, then weakened and plateaued, as shown in Fig. 4H, K, and 4N. This may be due to the abnormal death of bacteria in the process of culture, resulting in the failure of the fluorescence probe to identify bacteria, leading to reduction in fluorescence intensity.

By analyzing the Zeta potential experimental data (Figs. S7A, S7B and S7C), the three amino-modified aptamers were found to have positive charges, and the bacterial surface was found to have negative charges. The results showed that the neutralized charge of living bacteria was greater than that of dead bacteria, which proved that the recognition ability of aptamer was related to the state of bacteria (Chen et al., 2021). According to the circular dichroism analysis, S-apt had a positive peak at 264 nm to indicate the stacking degree of DNA base pairs, and a negative peak at 240 nm to indicate the degree of spiral porosity of DNA, which proves that S-apt has a typical G-quadruplex structure (Fig. S7D) (Wiedman et al., 2017). E-apt and P-apt had a negative peak at 240 nm and a positive peak at 277 nm, indicating that the two DNAs are right-handed helical B configurations (Figs. S7E and S7F). After the addition of live bacteria and dead bacteria, the live bacteria had higher signal response in the degree of weakening of the negative peak and the enhancement of the positive peak, which also indicated that living bacteria exert a greater effect on the conformation of aptamer than dead bacteria (Li et al., 2020). Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of the surface of the living bacteria in the fluorescence experimental data was greater than that of the dead bacteria (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of bacteria by sensors
ce:section.ce:para: For quantitative detection of bacteria, it was necessary to explore the optimal adsorption time and concentration of bacteria and the fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 5 A, B, and 5C, the fluorescence intensity of MIP adsorption was higher when the concentration of the bacterial solution was 1.0 × 105 CFU/mL, and after that the fluorescence intensity of MIP adsorption decreased. With the optimal adsorption time (S. aureus: 40 min, E. coli: 40 min, and P. aeruginosa: 30 min), the fluorescence intensity of MIP surface adsorption reached its maximum intensity (Fig. 5D, E, and 5F). The fluorescence intensity of the MIP surface decreased after this time because the death of bacteria reduced the probability of collision with MIP. Finally, the fluorescence intensity adsorbed on the MIP surface reached equilibrium. Then, the semi-quantitative analyses of the three target bacteria were performed by using the constructed fluorescence sensor platform and the image processing of the smartphone (Tang et al., 2023). The concentration range of the bacterial solution was 103–108 CFU/mL, and the data were fitted by the logarithm of the target bacteria's concentration (C) and the RGB value of the image. The following logarithmic regression models were obtained: G + B value = −3.5446 (Lg C) 2 + 72.783 Lg C + 113.79, R2 = 0.9936 (Fig. 5G); G + B value = −5.2079 (Lg C) 2 + 88.351 Lg C + 131.11, R2 = 0.9971 (Fig. 5H); and R + B value = 2.0216 (Lg C) 2 + 23.264 Lg C – 1.8587, R2 = 0.9914 (Fig. 5I). This indicates that there is a correlation between the concentration of the bacterial solution and RGB value, herein RGB analysis can be used for quantification of the specific bacteria in sample. Further the sensor was subjected to test with all three pathogens, and the data were fitted by the logarithm of the target bacteria's concentration (C) and the RGB value of the image. The fitting results of mixed bacteria were highly consistent with those of single bacteria (Fig. S9A), so the fitting curve of single bacteria can be used for subsequent experiments (Fig. S9B). The color distribution of each sample well can be used to determine whether the specific bacteria are present in mixture or not (Fig. 5J), and quantification can be realized by analysis of its corresponding RGB value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of fluorescent probes and molecularly imprinted materials
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the specificity of MIP and fluorescent probe, the adsorption capacity and recognition ability of molecularly imprinted materials and fluorescent probes for E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa, as well as for laboratory-screened B. subtilis, B. cereus, and B. methanolicus, were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5K, the fluorescence intensity of the three MIPs on the surface of the target bacteria was higher than that of the other bacteria. The fluorescence intensity on the surface of the three NIPs was weak, and cross-validating detection of pathogenic bacteria was carried out as well. In Fig. 5L, the fluorescence intensities of the fluorescent probe in the bacterial solutions of other bacteria and the Tris-HCl solution were low, whereas the fluorescence intensity in the target bacterial solution was very high. Thus, the results show that MIPs and fluorescent probes could achieve specific adsorption and detection of bacteria.

To further confirm the reliability of collaborative detection in the experiment, three kinds of target bacteria with different concentrations (103, 105, and 108 CFU/mL) were incubated with the fluorescent probe. After MIP adsorption, the unbound bacteria were washed with deionized water. A confocal microscope was used to take fluorescent images. As shown in Fig. S10, the fluorescence of MIP adsorption first increased and then slightly decreased along with the increase in the concentration of target bacteria, which was consistent with the experiment results presented in section 3.5.

As shown in Fig. S11, after incubating a mixture of bacteria (E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa) with three fluorescent probes, the probes combined with the bacteria and induced different fluorescence for each specific bacterial. After incubation in a magnetic field, the mixed bacterial solution exhibited different fluorescent colors in different areas due to differing MIP adsorption and enrichment. Because of the specificity and enrichment function of MIP, different bacteria will gather on the surface of the material with different fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity is further enhanced by the AIE properties of TPE derivatives (Niu et al., 2020), so much so that different bacteria may be distinguished by the naked eye (Huang et al., 2019). Each color has a specific RGB value, so the bacterial concentration can be calculated according to the fitting curve of the bacterial concentration and RGB values (Liu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of bacteria detection based on sensor readings
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the enrichment method was used to reduce the detection line and detection time of the sensor. Since the adsorption performance of MIP was much greater than the concentration of the added bacterial solution, the volume of the liquid to be measured was increased by 10 times, and a peristaltic pump was used for liquid circulation to further reduce the detection baseline. The experimental apparatus for cycling is shown in Fig. S12, and the comparison of experimental results after cycling is shown in Table S2. The figures in the Table S2 suggest that the results are also consistent with the fitting results described in section 3.5 with cyclic condition. After optimization, the detection time was reduced to 40 min and the detection limit was as low as 102 CFU/mL.

Samples of drinking water, orange juice, and milk were used to verify the feasibility of the method. First, 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL of different bacteria was added to drinking water, orange juice, and milk, and then each sample was diluted by 100 times. The RGB processing method of the portable sensor and the ordinary plate counting method were used to verify the accuracy of the experiment. As shown in Table S3, after the same batch of samples was processed using this fluorescent sensor, the error between the bacterial solution concentration calculated with RGB values and that calculated by the plate counting was approximately 10%. As shown in Table 1 , the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.059–5.518% and the recovery was 82.33%–92.77%, which indicated that this detection strategy had good accuracy and stability. Compared with other previously reported (Table S4; Wang et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2019; Wen et al., 2022), this work applies sensor can realize the quantitative and qualitative analysis of bacterial in real samples without relying on other detection devices.

In this study, we presented a simple smartphone-based fluorescent sensor to rapidly and sensitively detect E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa, providing quantitative detection of these three pathogenic bacteria from 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 108 CFU/mL in 40 min. The main novelty of this work was the independently designed 3D model, which was used to enable MIP and fluorescent probes to achieve specific adsorption and detection of bacteria. The bacterial detection procedures, including incubation, enrichment, separation, washing, and detection, were carried out in the 3D model. The proposed sensor exhibited the features of low cost, high sensitivity, good specificity, easy operation, fast response, and free equipment. The sensor can be applied to detect the target bacteria in juice, milk, and water.

However, the proposed sensor also has some limitations, such as few types of test samples and not fully automated during the detection process. For practical use purpose, the types of MIPs and fluorescent probes should be expanded and the detection area size and number should be optimized to realize the simultaneous detection of more bacteria. Overall, the sensor not only serves as an alternative and attractive platform for the detection of E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa, but also provides a universal model for detecting diverse targets in actual samples.

Pengchao Yin: Writing – original draft. Jing Wang: Conceptualization. Ting Li: Software, Visualization. Qingbin Pan: Data curation. Linchen Zhu: Methodology. Feifei Yu: Validation. Yong-zhen Zhao: Formal analysis. Hai-Bo Liu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC Grant No. 22167003, 21966006) and the Hundred-Talent Program (Guangxi Province).
